Repository: animesh/scripts — Copilot instructions

Purpose

This file helps GitHub Copilot CLI sessions interact productively with this repository by documenting build/test/lint commands, high-level architecture, and repository-specific conventions.

Startup instruction:

- Before editing anything, make sure to read and understand `copilot_preferences.md`.
- Use `cmd.exe` quoting for command execution to avoid PowerShell heredoc quoting issues.
- Prefer command line output shown directly in terminal output when validating script runs.


Build, test, and lint commands

- Docker (build the image provided in the repo):
  - Build: docker build -t animesh1977/scripts .
  - Run interactively: docker run --rm -it -v %CD%:/work -w /work animesh1977/scripts /bin/bash

- Python type checking (pyright):
  - Run on the whole repo: npx pyright
  - Run on a single file: npx pyright path\to\file.py
  - Note: pyrightconfig.json at repo root is permissive (many reports disabled).

- Run a single script (examples):
  - Python: python path\to\script.py
  - Perl: perl path\to\script.pl
  - R: Rscript path/to/script.R
  - MATLAB (non-interactive): matlab -batch "run('path/to/script.m')"

High-level architecture (big picture)

- Flat "scripts" repository: contains a large collection of small-to-medium standalone scripts across many languages. Files are grouped by filename prefixes (see Key conventions below) rather than deep directories.
- Primary languages: Perl (extensive parsing and automation), Python (analysis/ML/automation), R/Rmd (statistics, plotting, reports), MATLAB (.m numerical/analysis), shell scripts (.sh) and Windows batch files (.bat).
- Many scripts are self-contained and intended to be executed directly; dependencies are often documented inline or in per-script comments.
- Dockerfile: builds a Debian-based image, installs dotnet-sdk-8.0 and expects a diann-$DIANNV directory to be copied in (see Dockerfile). Use Docker to provide a reproducible environment for tools needing native/binary dependencies.
- There is no single monorepo build/test harness; validation is typically done by running the script(s) directly or inside the Docker image.

Key conventions (repo-specific patterns)

- File-name clusters reflect functionality. Examples include prefixes like:
  - mqrun*  (mass spectrometry/workflow orchestration)
  - proteinGroups* / peptideGroups* (protein/peptide downstream processing)
  - diffExpr* (differential expression analyses and plots)
- Cross-platform variants: many utilities have both .sh (POSIX) and .bat or helper scripts for Windows.
- Perl-first parsing: a large portion of text-processing and ETL-style code is implemented in Perl — treat Perl scripts as primary parsing/ingest utilities unless a Python/R counterpart is present.
- Pyright usage: pyrightconfig.json exists and is intentionally permissive (many diagnostics disabled). Running pyright is safe but will not enforce strict typing by default.
- Git CRLF: README recommends setting core.autocrlf true for Windows users (git config --global core.autocrlf true).

Quick troubleshooting / common commands

- Search for scripts by prefix: (from repo root) dir /b /s mqrun*   (Windows) or git ls-files | grep mqrun
- Run a single Python file: python scripts\example.py
- Type-check a single Python file: npx pyright scripts\example.py
- Reproduce environment in Docker: docker build -t mylocal/scripts . && docker run --rm -it -v %CD%:/work -w /work mylocal/scripts /bin/bash

Notes for Copilot sessions

- Prefer looking for filename clusters when asked about functionality (e.g., search for proteinGroups* rather than relying on one file).
- When suggesting edits, be conservative: many scripts are legacy and language-specific — prefer minimal, well-scoped changes and include tests or a manual verification step (running the script) in the PR description.
- If proposing a new CI workflow or linters, recommend adding opt-in scripts per language (e.g., pyproject/requirements, a small test harness) rather than imposing a single tooling strategy.

Files referenced while generating these notes:
- README.md
- Dockerfile
- pyrightconfig.json
